text: BEERSHEBA, or BIR-ES-SEBA, two wells in the Wadi-es-Seba, in the pash. of Gaza, 27 m. SW of Hebron, and about the same distance SE of Gaza, nearly midway between the S extremity of the Dead sea and the Mediterranean, supposed to mark the site of the ancient Beersheba.—Robinson.—Wilson.

BEES (SAINT), a parish in Cumberland, 3 m. W by N of Egremont, stretching along the coast, and comprising the port and town of Whitehaven, the chapelries of Ennerdale, Eskdale, Wasdale, and Nether- Wasdale, and the townships of St. Bees, Hensingham, Kinneyside, Lowside-Quarter, Preston-Quarter, Rottington, Sandwith, and Wheddicar. The v. was formerly known by the name of Begock, Begoth, or Beghes. Area 69,260 acres. Pop. in 1801, 12,848; in 1841, 19,087. The eastern part of the abbey, built in the 13th cent., was fitted up in 1817 as a college, containing one large hall for the students, and a lecture-room, the end of the ancient cross-aisle being converted into another. This institution was commenced under the auspices of the late Dr. G. H. Law, bishop of Chester, for the benefit of young men in the northern dioceses, who do not intend to complete their education in Oxford or Cambridge, and who become entitled to receive ordination after having studied for a certain time at this place. Students, previous to admission, are expected to be well-versed in the classics, so that the course of study does not exceed two years, or at most three. The parish contains coal-mines; lime and freestone are also found in abundance; and at Kinneyside there is a leadmine. The district forms a promontory on the coast, and appears, from various ruins, to have been fortified by the Romans, at all convenient landing-places, against the incursions of the Irish and Scots. The v. is very ancient, and stands on the side of the bay which is formed by the southern headland.—St. Bees' head anciently called the cliff of Barath, a conspicuous sea-mark for vessels, is in N lat. 54° 31', W long. 3° 39'; and has a lighthouse, erected in 1718, with a fixed light, elevated 333 ft. above high water, and visible at the distance of 20 m. in clear weather.
BEESBY. See HAWERBY.
BEESBY-IN-THE-MARSH, a parish in Lincolnshire, 3 m. N by E of Alford. Area 1,180 acres. Pop. 157. BEG
BEESEL, or Belsel, a commune of the Netherlands, in the prov. of Limburg, 5 m. NE of Roermond. Pop. 1,228.

BEESKOW, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Brandenburg, and circle of Lubben, situated on the l. bank of an expansion of the Spree, 18 m. SW of Frankfurt, and 40 m. SE of Berlin, on the post road from that town to Vienna. It has manufactories of cloth, linen, and tobacco, breweries, tanneries, and lime-kilns. Pop. in 1837, 3,406.
BEESTEN, a town of Hanover, in the prov. and 11 m. SE of Lingen, on the l. bank of the Aa, 5 m. above the confluence of that river with the Ems.

BEESTON, a hamlet in the parishes of Northill and Sandy in Bedfordshire, 21 m NNW of Biggleswade. Pop. in 1841, 406.—Also a township in the p. of Bunbury, Cheshire. In the vicinity are the ruins of an ancient fortress, situated on an insulated rock, 366 ft. in height, destroyed by order of parliament in 1645. Area 1,720 acres. Pop. 426.- Also a parish in Norfolkshire, 6½ m. NE of Swaffham. Area 3,100 acres. Pop. 661.—Also a parish and v. in Nottinghamshire, 4 m. SW of Nottingham. Area 1,440 acres. Pop. with the hamlet of Beeston-Ryelands, 2,807. The Midland counties railway has a station in this p., 3 m. from Nottingham, and 13 m. from Derby.—Also a chapelry in the p. of St. Peter, in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 21 m. S by W of Leeds. Area 1,770 acres. Pop. 2,175.
BEESTON (St. Andrew), a parish in Norfolk, 41 m. NNE of Norwich. Area 920 acres. Pop. 46.
BEESTON (St. LAWRENCE), a parish in Norfolk, 3½ m. ENE of Coltishall. Area 450 acres. Pop. 48.
BEESTON-REGIS, a parish in Norfolk, 3 m. WNW of Cromer. Area 740 acres. Pop. 265.
BEETSER ZWAAG, a town of Holland, in the prov. of Friesland, 16 m. SE of Lieuwarden.
BEETELFACKIE. See BEITEL-FAKIH.
BEETHAM, a parish in Westmoreland, on Morecombe bay, 8 m. W of Kirkby-Lonsdale. Area 12,850 acres. Pop. 1,656, of whom 845 were in the township of B.
BEETLEY, a parish in Norfolk, 4 m. NW by N of East Dereham. Area 1,770 acres. Pop. 394.
BEFORT. See Belfort.

BEG (Lough), an expansion of the Lower Bann, between co. Antrim and co. Londonderry. It commences about 1 m. below the exit of the river from Lough Neagh, and has a length of about 4 m. with a mean breadth of from 4 to 6 furlongs.

BEGA (ALT), a river of Hungary, an affluent of the Danube. It rises in the E part of the Krassoer com., and flows through Temeswar and Torontal, falling into the Danube to the E of the conflux of the Theiss. A canal 74 m. in length unites the B. to the Theiss, by substituting a new bed running nearly in a straight line from Fakset to Becskerek, for the old and winding bed of the B. This is sometimes called the New B. or the Temesk canal.
BEGAAR, a commune of France, in the dep. of Landes, and cant. of Tartas. Pop. 1,141.
BEGAH (UL), a town of Arabia, in the prov. of Nedjed-el-Aredh, 150 m. NW of Lahsa.
BEGADAN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gironde, and cant. of Lesparre. Pop. 1,176.
BEGANNE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, and cant. of Allaire. Pop. 1,399.

BEGARD, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Cotes-du-Nord, arrond. of Guingamp. The cant. comprises 7 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 10,428. The vil. is 9 m. NW of Guingamp. Pop. 3,768.
BEG-BAZARI, a village of Anatolia, in the sanjak of Enguri, W of Ayash. There are warm baths in the vicinity.
BEGBROOKE, a parish in Oxfordshire, 3 m. SE by S of Woodstock. Area 380 acres. Pop. 110.

BEGELLY, a parish in Pembrokeshire, 5 m. SSE of Narberth. Pop. 1,159. BEGEMDER, [i. e. 'Sheep country,'] a province of Abyssinia, to the SE of the Lake of Dembea. It stretches from Empras on the N, to the Abai on the S, and is watered by several tributaries of the Abai. It is less mountainous than some other districts of the kingdom, and abounds in luxuriant pasturage, affording maintenance to immense numbers of horses, sheep, and cattle. Yet its elevation is considerable, as the height of Lake Dembea itself is 6,110 ft. above sea-level. Extensive iron mines exist in some localities. This prov. suffers from frequent incursions of the adjacent Galla tribes.
BEG-ERIN, an island off the coast of co. Wexford, 13 m. W of the N side of the entrance of Wexford harbour. It is <math>1\frac{1}{4}</math> m. in length.
BEGGAR'S BRIDGE, or ROCHFORT BRIDGE, a village in the parish of Castlelost, in co. Westmeath, 7½ m. SW by W of Kinnegad. Pop. 417.
BEGGAR'S BUSH, a village in the p. of Donnybrook, co. Leinster. Pop. 855.
BEGGE MUXUD, or MEXUD-BEGGY, a town of Persia, in the prov. and 65 m. SE of Ispahan.

BEGGENRIED, or BECKENRIED, a parish of Switzerland, in the cant. of Unterwalden, on the S bank of the Waltstatter-see, 5 m. E of Stanz. It has a good port and building docks, and possesses an active trade. Pop. 1,500.
BEGGINGEN, a parish of Switzerland, in the cant. of Schaffhausen, and district of Schleitheim, at the base of the Randenberg. Pop. 1,208.
BEGHARMI. See BAGARMEH.

BEGHRAM, or Begram, [i. e., according to Masson, 'Chief city,'] a plain covered with mud-built ruins, 25 m. N of the city of Kabul, at the SE point of the Kohistan, in Afghanistan. A vast number of Greek, Roman, Bactrian, Parthian, Scythian, and Sassanian coins and seals have been found here. Masson is of opinion that the ruins are those of the Alexandria ad Caucasum of the Greeks. This opinion is supported by Major Rawlinson; but Ritter, Rennell, and Burnes identify Bamian with that ancient city. The present Hindus call the site Babram, and suppose it to have been the cap. of Raja Bal.
BEGIA. See BEJA.
BEG-INNES, an island on the N entrance of Valencia harbour, off the coast of co. Kerry. It is about 2 m. in circumf.
BEGLAISEH, or BIGLAISH, a town of Turkey in Asia, in the pash. of Sivas, and sanjak of Buzok, 45 m. WNW of Uskat.
BEGLES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gironde, and cant. of Bordeaux, on the Garonne, 3 m. S of Bordeaux. Pop. 2,322.
BEGLI. See Benli.
BEGNEZZIN, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Khorassan, in the valley of Mushed, 35 m. W of Radcan, and 60 m. NW of Mushed.
BEGNINS, or Begnin, a parish of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, 5 m. N of Nion. Pop. 645.

BEGONA, a village of Spain, in the prov. of Biscay, nearly 1 m. SE of Bilboa. The parish church is finely situated on a commanding eminence. The vil. contains several tanneries, potteries, iron forges, and a rope-work.—Also a village 9 m. NNW of Bilboa, at the embouchure of the Ansa. It has a small fort for the defence of the adjacent coast.

BEG-SHEHR, or Bei-Shehr, a town of Turkey in Asia, cap. of the sanjak of the same name, in the prov. of Karamania, situated in a plain on the eastern side of Lake Beg-shehr, 45 m. SW of Konia. It has a castle built by the sultan Aladan, two BEH mosques, and the same number of baths. The sanjak of B. is bounded on the NW by the sanjak of Ak-shehr; on the NE and E by Konia; on the S by the sanjak of Itshili. It consists of a valley enclosed on three sides by branches of the Taurus chain, and containing the lakes of Beg-shehr and Jede-shehr, the desert of Khan Cazan, and several thermal springs. The soil is to a great extent fertile and well-watered. The mountain acclivities are bare and rocky, and their summits covered during a great portion of the year with snow.-The lake of B. is 30 or 40 m. in circumference; but of variable extent according to the season. Its waters are saline, and abound with fish.

BEGU (CAPE), a lofty headland of Spain, on the NE coast of the prov. of Catalonia, at the southern embouchure of the Ter, in N lat. 41° 56' 38", and E long. 3° 9′ 30". It is surmounted by a tower, and shelters on the W and SW the Cove of Agua-Fria or Begu, in which there is a depth of from 9 to 13 fathoms.
BEGUDE-DE-JORDY (LA), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Herault, cant. and com. of Servian. Pop. 57.
BEGUDE-DE-SAZE (LA), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Gard, cant. of Villeneuve-les-Avignon, and com. of Saze. Pop. 20.
BEGUEY NEYRAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gironde, and cant. of Cadillac. It affords good common wines. Pop. 958.
BEGUILDY, a parish in Radnorshire, 8 m. NW of Knighton, on the Teme. Pop. 1,051, of whom 601 were in Upper B.
BEGUMABAD, a village of Hindostan, in the prov. and 25 m. NE of Delhi, in the dist. of Murut. In the vicinity is an extensive serai.
BEGUM-SOMROV. See SEERDHUNA.

BEGYG, a village of Central Egypt, in the prov. of Faioum, and 3 m. SW of Medinet-el-Faioum. It is supposed to occupy the site of the ancient Arsinoe or Crocodilopolis, and contains amongst other ruins a fine granite obelisk distinguished by its cylindrical summit.

BEHABAN, or BIHBAHAN, a district and town of Persia, in the prov. of Khuzistan.—The town is on the l. bank of the Jerahi or Sherahi, 150 m. SE of Shuster. It is in a fertile and extensive plain, about 20 m. in length. It is surrounded by a mud wall about 3 m. in circuit; but the town itself is almost a heap of ruins, and does not contain above 4,000 inhabitants.
BEHAR. See BAHAR.
BEHAR. See AKHARSU.
BEHAR KATRA, a fortified town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Malwa, situated in a defile on the l. bank of the Parbutty river. Pop. in 1820, 1,100.

BEHBEYT, or BEHBIT, a town of Lower Egypt, in the prov. of Garbieh, 6 m. W of Mansurah. It occupies the site of the ancient Anysis, or Isidis Oppidum, amid the ruins of which are those of a magnificent temple dedicated to Isis.
BEHDUROO, a small and nearly unknown district of Hindostan, in the Kohistan of Lahore, to the N of Chamba.
BEHNESEH. See BAHNASAH.
BEHIRAT-EL-MERDJI. See BAHR-EL-MERJ.
BEHM'S CANAL, a narrow strait which separates the island of Revillagigedo in the New Cornwall group from the W coast of British America, extending 54 m. in length, within the 56th N parallel.

BEHNAU, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of Brandenburg, and circle of Sorau, 57 m. SSE of Frankfurt. It has some linen manufactories. The environs produce excellent fruit, and afford considerable quantities of wine. Pop. 1,210.
BEHNECE. See BAHNASAH.
BEHOBIE, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Basses-Pyrénées, cant. of St. Jean-de-Luz, and com. of Urrugne, at the pass of the Pyrenees, by which the road from Bayonne enters Spain.
BEHONE, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Allahabad and dist. of Bundelcund, 12 m. NE of Rajnagur.
BEHONNE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Meuse, and cant. of Vavincourt. It affords good common wines. Pop. 585.

BEHRAM, a district of Kurdistan, to the S of Fahliyan, inhabited by Mamaseni tribes, and watered by a small river, flowing NW. There are numerous ruins, and some curious sculptures, scattered over this region.
BEHREE, or Beree, a town and pergunnah of Hindostan, in the prov. of Agra, on the l. bank of the Betwa, 19 m. SE of Kalpy or Calpie.

BEHRENDT, or BERN, a town of W Prussia, cap. of the circle of the same name, in the gov. and 33 m. SW of Danzig. It contains 4 churches; and possesses manufactories of linen, breweries, and distilleries. Pop. in 1837, 1,713.—The circle, which comprises an area of 192 m., is covered with forests and lakes. Pop. 16,817. BEHRENTHAL, BAERENTHAL, or BERENTHAL, a commune of France, in the dep. of Moselle, and cant. of Bitche. It contains extensive iron-forges and steel-works. Pop. 1,229. BEHRING'S (or BEERING'S) ISLAND, an island in the North Pacific ocean, classed with the Aleutian chain by some geographers, but by others excluded from it. It is 100 m. SE of Cape Kamtschatka; and extends 104 m. in length, by 15 m. in breadth. Its N point is in N lat. 55° 22', E long. 165° 51'. It appears from sea "a moderately high island armed with rocky points." [Beechey.] The W coasts are elevated; the S is low land. The principal mountains, called the Stanavoy ridge, consist of granite and sandstone. The lower parts of the island are covered with moss, but totally destitute of shrubs. There are two bays on the coast, wherein vessels engaged in the fur-trade have been accustomed to winter, but they are shallow, of dangerous access, and exposed to the N winds. Snow never melts on some of the mountains. Several small streams issue from inconsiderable lakes or pools near the shore. Minerals of value are said to have been found, and pieces of native copper are cast ashore after violent storms. The surrounding seas abound in whales; seals are numerous on the shores, and multitudes of sea-otters, black and blue foxes, formerly inhabited the island. The sea-cow was at one time an object of pursuit to the fishers visiting this island; but the species is either extinct, or deterred from approaching the island, as none have been seen on it since the year 1708. When the sea-otter, whose numbers have also greatly diminished, disappears in March, it is succeeded here by the sea-lion.—This island was discovered in 1740, or 1741, by Vitus Behring or Beering, a Dane, and a commodore in the Russian service. While engaged in a voyage of discovery, he approached it under circumstances of great distress; his vessel was totally wrecked on its shores; and many of the crew fell victims to a series of hardships encountered upon it during the succeeding winter; M. de la Croyere, a French astronomer, died on the island, and Behring himself at length yielded to the effects of disease and privations. The island was fatal to an English vessel at a later period, in the course of navigating from India for the fur trade of the North, in 1787, or 1788. It is totally uninhabited. BEHRING'S (or Beering's) STRAITS, the narrow sea between the W coast of North America, and I. BEI the E coast of Asia, so called from Behring, who, with Tshirikow, sailed from Kamtschatka in quest of the New World. They both discovered land within a few degrees of the NW coast of America, and the more recent discoveries of Cook, Kotzebue, and Beechey, have confirmed the near approach of the two continents. Those straits at their nearest approach in N lat. 66° were ascertained by Captain Cook to be 13 leagues wide, beyond which they diverge to the NE by E and WNW; in N lat. 69° they become about 100 leagues asunder. A great similarity is observable in the appearance of the two countries, to the northward of the straits; both are destitute of wood; the shores of both are low, with mountains rising to a great height farther up the country. The depth of water mid-way between them is 29 and 30 fathoms, decreasing gradually as either continent is approached, with the difference of being somewhat shoaler on the American than on the Asiatic coast, at the same distance from land. The bottom in the middle is a soft slimy mud; on drawing near to either shore, it is found to be a brown sand, intermixed with small fragments of bones, and a few shells. No great tide is perceptible; what there is comes from the westward. The prevailing current sets to the N. About midway across between Prince of Wales cape and East cape, are the islands called the Dromedes, the largest of which, Ratmanoff, is about 3 m. in length, and high to the S.
BEHRUNGEN, a town of Saxe-Meiningen, in the prin. and 15 m. S of Meiningen, and 12 m. W of Hildburghausen. It has several annual fairs. Pop. 560.

BEHUT, a town and pergunnah of Hindostan, in the prov. of Allahabad, and dist. of Bundelcund, near the r. bank of the Dessaum, 17 m. NW of Jeitpoor. -Also a town in the prov. of Delhi, in the dist. and 15 m. NNE of Scharaunpoor.
BEHTINIDEH. See BATINDAH.
BEIAD, or BEYADIE, a town of Upper Egypt, inhabited by Christians, 24 m. ENE of Benisuef.
BEIAT, a town of Turkey in Asia, in the sanjak of Karahissar, on the road from Konia to the coast of the Black sea, 50 m. SE of Kutaya.

BEIBAZER, or BEY-BAZAR, a town of Turkey in Asia, in Anatolia, cap. of the sanjak of Sultan Oghi, 57 m. W of Angora. It is situated on two steep hills near the r. bank of the Kirmir, 12 m. above the confluence of that river with the Sakaria. The town consists of about 1,000 houses, and contains some carpet manufactories. The environs are fertile, and produce grain, including rice and cotton. Goats and sheep are extensively reared in the surrounding district. Their hair and wool form important articles of commerce with Angora.
BEIDA DAREL. See ANAFA.
BEIDHAM (EL), a river of Syria, in the pash. of Damascus, which flows into the Jordan 20 m. ESE of Nablous.

BEIDARY, or BAIDARY, a valley of Russia, in the S of the Crimea. It is watered by the Targouna, and bathed on the S by the Black sea; and from its beauty and fertility, strikingly contrasting with the wildness of the adjacent mountain district, has been called the Tempe of the Taurida. It contains 5 villages.

BEIERTHEIM, a village of Baden, in the circle of the Middle Rhine, and bailiwick of Carlsruhe, on the Alb. In the vicinity are the much frequented mineral springs and baths of Stephanienbad. Pop. 583.
BEIGHNOU, a commune of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, and cant. of Guer. Pop. 1,569.

BEIGHTON, a parish in Derbyshire, 9 m. NE of Chesterfield. Area 3,070 acres. Pop. 1,121.—Also a p. in Norfolk, 3 m. SW of Arle. Area 910 acres. 28 Pop. 288.—Also a p. in Suffolk, 5 m. E by S of Bury St. Edmonds. Area 550 acres. Pop. 384.

BEIKEM, a river of Mongolia, in the dist. of Ouliassoutai, which takes its rise in the SE flank of the Ergik-targak range, passes through Lake Kara- Balak, and unites with the Kamsara, one of the principal head-streams of the Yenisei, in N lat. 51° 40'.

BEIKERMAN, a town of Russia, in the Crimea, and dist. of Simferopol, in the vicinity of which is a quarry of excellent soap-stone, large quantities of which are exported to Constantinople by way of Balaklava.
BEIKIRI, a town of Turkey in Asia, in the gov. of Karamania, and sanjak of Beg-shehr, 53 m. WSW of Konia.
BEILA. See BELA.

BEILA'N, or BYLAN, a town of Syria, in the pash. of Aleppo, 9 m. from the coast of the Mediterranean, and about the same distance SE of Iskenderun. It is situated near the summit level of a gorge of the mountain of the same name, formed by a ramification of the Taurus chain, at an alt. of 1,584 ft. above sea-level. The streets run along the brink of lofty precipices, and are watered by numerous streamlets which descend to the river—supposed to be the Kersus of Xenophon-in the valley below. The houses are intermingled with gardens of great luxuriance and beauty. The climate is remarkable for its salubrity. Pop. 4,000. Numerous cattle are reared; and tobacco in small quantities is cultivated in the environs. This town was during the prosperity of Iskenderun much frequented by Europeans. It was the scene of a victory of Ibrahim Pasha over the Turks in 1832.—The Beilan mountain has an alt. of 5,337 ft.
BEILBY, a chapelry in the p. of Hayton, E. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. S of Pocklington. Area 1,220 acres. Pop. 273.
BEILEN, a town of Holland, in the prov. of Drenthe, on the l. bank of the Havelter river, and 11 m. S of Assen.
BEILKEIRA, a village of Hindostan, in the prov. of Kandeish, 50 m. SSW of Burhampur, possessing a good stone-built fort.

BEILNGRIES, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Regen, near the confluence of the Sulz and Altmühl, 17 m. NE of Eichstadt, and 29 m. W of Regensburg. It consists of about 150 houses, and is celebrated for the manufacture of clocks and watches. B. was erected into a town in 1480. Cattle are reared in great numbers in the environs. Pop. 1,100.
BEILSINAGUR, or BILSUAGUR, a town and fort of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bahar, and zemindary of Chutanagpoor, 30 m. SE of Deosah.

BEILSTEIN, a town of Nassau, 8 m. N of Weilburg.—Also a town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Neckar, bail. and 6 m. NNE of Marbach. It is situated in a mountainous district, at an alt. of 820 ft. above sea-level. It is of considerable antiquity, and is celebrated for its mineral baths. The vine is extensively cultivated in the environs. Pop. 1,218.—Also a town of Prussia, in the prov. of the Lower Rhine, on the r. bank of the Moselle, 22 m. SW of Coblentz. It formerly belonged to Count Metternich Beilstein. Pop. 300.
BEIN. See BEN.
BEINA, a river of Norway, rising in numerous lakes and streams to the S of the Syndest-field, and E of the Fille-field, and flowing in a SSE course to the Tyri-fiord.
BEINDER, a town of Turkey in Asia, in Anatolia, in the sanjak and 30 m. SE of Smyrna. - Also a town in the sanjak and 40 m. E of Boli, on the l. bank of the Olu or Barton river.
BEINE, a canton and commune of France, in the BEI dep. of Marne, and arrond. of Reims. The cant. comprises 19 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 10,494. The com. is 9 m. E of Reims. Pop. 1,019.
BEINETTE, a town of Piedmont, in the prov. and 6 m. ESE of Coni. It has several paper-mills. Pop. 800.
BEINHEIM, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Bas-Rhin, and cant. of Seltz, situated near the Zorn, 27 m. NNE of Strasburg, and 15 m. SE of Wissemburg. Pop. 1,545.
BEINSTEIN, a village of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Neckar, on the r. bank of the Reins, 9 m. ENE of Stuttgard, and 10 m. SE of Louisburg. Pop. 965.
BEINT, or Bunth, a town of Beluchistan, near the source of the river of the same name, 110 m. SSW of Bunpur.

BEINWYL, or Beinweil, a parish and v. in the Swiss cant. of Argovia, in the bailiwick of Muri. Pop. 808, Catholics.—Also a parish and v. in the cant. of Soleure, and bailiwick of Dorneck-Thierstein, in a valley of the Jura to which it gives name. Pop. 384, Catholics. A convent of Benedictines was founded here in 1085.

BEIRA, or BEYRA, a province of Portugal, bounded on the N by the province of Tras-os-Montes and Entre Duero e Minho, from which it is separated by the river Douro; on the E by Spain; on the S by Portuguese Estremadura and Alentejo; and on the W by the Atlantic. The length of its sea-coast is about 80 m. Its area is commonly computed at 11,000 sq. m. Ebeling estimates it 7,355, and Franzini at 6,480, Italian sq. m. The pop. in 1810 was 880,602; in 1822, it was estimated at 1,067,640; in 1835, at 1,195,560. It is divided into Alta Beira and Baixa Beira, or Upper and Lower Beira: the former comprising that portion of the country which lies between the Douro river and the Sierra de Estrella; and the latter the remaining portion between the Sierra de Estrella and the Tagus. These are subdivided into 11 civil jurisdictions called comarcas, and 7 bishoprics, viz., those of Coimbra, Lamego, Vizeu, Guarda, Castello-Branco, Aveiro, and Pinhel, with what is called the liberty of Santa Cruz. This prov. is full of mountains, particularly in the E, where it is traversed by the Sierra de Estrella, which runs from NE to SW, and attains an alt. of 7,524 ft. above sea-level. Another chain, called the Sierra de Alcoba, runs parallel to this range from the Douro to Cape Mondego. These give rise to a multitude of rivers, of which the principal are the Vouga, the Mondego, and the Zezere. Rich specimens of silver ore have been found in the mountains between Vizeu and Lamego; and it is apparent that both silver and lead mines have been wrought in these mountains in former ages; but after the discovery of the New World, the Portuguese were prohibited by their sovereigns from even searching for the precious metals in their native country. The valleys are rich in pasture, and yield quantities of excellent wine. Corn, however, is hardly raised in sufficient quantity for home-consumption: its place is in some measure supplied by chestnuts. The olive-plantations are numerous and productive; the oil obtained from them forms, with fruit, the principal article of export. The cheese made in the Sierra de Estrella is highly celebrated. The valley of the Mondego, lying between the two principal mountain-ranges above noticed, is the richest and most picturesque district in the prov.—The infant Don Pedro, eldest son of prince Joseph Francis Xavier, was the first that bore the title of prince of Beira.
BEIRA (VILLA DA PRINCESA DA). See CAMPANHA.
BEIRAMITSCHI, a town of Anatolia, on the l. bank of the Mendere Chai, 30 m. NW by W of | pashalik of Acre only in the early part of this cent. Adramyti.

BEIRAMKOI, a town of Anatolia, on the N coast of the gulf of Adramyti, 40 m. W by S of Adramyti. BEIRUT, BEYROUT, or BAIRUT, anciently Berytus, a seaport town of Syria, in the pashalik of Acre, on an elevated promontory bathed by the sea on three sides, in N lat. 33° 49½', E long. 35° 27', about 30 m. N. of Sidon. There was formerly a harbour here which is now choked up with sand and rubbish; nothing of it is seen but a pier, apparently of ancient construction, which shelters the boats of the country, while merchant vessels and ships-of-war anchor at a distance in the open roadsted. The town is surrounded by a wall, built by Djezzar Pasha, after the place was bombarded by the Russians, to give it a more formidable appearance. With the same view he pulled down a high tower to the NE, and rebuilt it in a more substantial manner. The streets of B. are narrow and irregular; and the suburbs are nearly as large as the town, consisting of houses interspersed with gardens, which being planted with fruit-trees have a pleasing appearance. The environs also are agreeable; they are laid out in plantations chiefly of mulberries; and a stream—the Nahr-el-Beirut—descending from Mount Lebanon to the sea, winds through the country. Vines and orange-trees abound; but palms and cactuses are the vegetable productions which most attract the eye of a European. The pop. amounts to about 12,000; and is composed of Druses, Maronites, Jews, Greeks, Turks, Negroes, and Arabs. B. is the residence of a Greek and likewise of a Maronite bishop. The cultivation of silk spreads rapidly in the vicinity of B.; the sugar-cane is also cultivated. Wine—for which the town was famous of old—is no longer made here; but the inhabitants are supplied from the neighbouring mountains. The staple commodity of the country is raw silk of fine quality which is carried to Cairo, Aleppo, and Damascus, and partly to Europe. Cotton, olives, and figs, also form articles of export. Earthen jars and jugs of a particular kind, manufactured at B., are much esteemed, from the nature of the clay of which they are formed. The charge for land-carriage from B. to Damascus is from 80 to 100 piastres per cantar. The post goes to Constantinople in 7 days in winter, and in 5 days in summer. The export-trade to Europe is chiefly managed by means of French and Italian merchants; and consists of cottons, galls, silks, gums, madder, and wool. The total export of silk from B. in the four years from 1833 to 1836, was 4,811 bales, weighing from 195 to 210 lbs. each, and valued at 8,493,533 francs. Of this the total value exported to England was only 93,220 francs. B., however, is a valuable entrepot for English manufactures, particularly hardware and cottons. This place is an emporium to which the Druses and Maronites send their products; in return they receive rice, tobacco, coffee, and specie, which they exchange for corn. It is considered the chief town of the Druses, and the healthiest town on a coast subject to miasmatic influences, as all the low district between Lebanon and the Mediterranean is. The climate is subject to less change than that of either Algiers or Alexandria, but the therm. generally ranges from 85° to 90° during the day. Four granite columns, -of which three are within the precincts of the town, -and the remains of other magnificent buildings, attest its former grandeur and extent. Agrippa, the grandson of Herod the Great, constructed a theatre and amphitheatre here, as well as baths. From the quay several ruins of walls are to be seen standing in the water, and the greater part of the landing-place is a pile of granite and marble columns. B. remained in the possession of the Druses of a considerable period, and was united to the 34 m. N of Chamir. BEI Djezzar Pasha benefited the place, by bringing in several fountains which supply it with abundance of water in all seasons; but the fortifications which he erected could be of little utility in the event of an attack, as they are commanded by the adjacent grounds. Towards the land it is enclosed by a wall. The road from Sidon to B. runs along Mount Lebanon, which here rises steeply from the coast; between B. and Tripoli it proceeds along the foot of the mountains, which rise in an amphitheatre of successive terraces from the shore. There is the appearance of a large pop. along the shores and mountain-sides about B.; villages, patches of cultivation, mosques, and marabuts are numerous.
BEISLEIDEN, a village of Prussia, in the gov. of Königsberg, SE of Eylau. Pop. 143.

BEIT, or BETH, or, softening the sibilant, BET, an Arabic word signifying 'house,' and of frequent occurrence in Oriental topography, especially in that of Palestine and Arabia. It is also used to signify a 'tribe,' 'clan,' or 'sept.'
BEIT 'AINU'N, a hamlet in Syria, a few miles N of Hebron, supposed to mark the site of the ancient Bethanoth. There are some ruins here, and prostrate columns .- Biblioth. Sac., vol. i.

BEITAVEND, or BEITAWAND, a village of Persia. in Khuzistan, 15 m. NE of Shuster. It is on a small stream, an affluent of the Shurish, which flows NW by N to the Karun, and is surrounded by green fields and meadows. It consists of about 100 neat and clean houses.—De Bode.—Layard.
BEIT DEJAN, a village of Syria, on the road from Lydda to Joppa. It probably marks the site of an ancient Beth-Dagon, or 'House of Dagon.'—Robinson.
BEIT-ED-EIN, a village of Syria, 12 m. distant from Deir-el-Kamar. A magnificent palace was built here by the late Emir Beshir.
BEIT-EL-DJANNE, or Beit-Jenn, a village of Syria, on the road from Damascus to Cæsarea Philippi, 10 m. ENE of the latter place.

BEIT-EMIREEN, a village of Syria, one hour from Sebaste or Samaria. It consists of about 40 houses, seated on a rising ground, on a chalky soil, and surrounded by valleys thickly wooded with olivetrees.—Buckingham.

BEIT-EL-FAKIH, a town of Arabia, 24 m. ESE of Locheia, in N lat. 14° 31', celebrated for its trade in coffee, which is grown on a range of hills about 16 m. distant. It is situated in a sandy but wellcultivated plain, and is governed by a bombishi with a garrison of 500 men. The dwellings are all detached, and the greater part mere huts. In the centre is a citadel of some strength. Cruttenden estimated the pop. in 1836 at 8,000. Besides coffee, wax, gums, and frankincense, are here exchanged for piece-goods, from India, English shawls, spices, and sugar. A heavy duty is here levied on all kafilahs that arrive from Sanaa on their way to Hodeida or Mokha.—Niebuhr.—Cruttenden.

BEIT-HANINAH, a village of Syria, on the road from Jerusalem to Tiberias, which gives name to a well-marked wadi, the counterpart of that of Jehoshaphat, and running in the opposite direction towards the Mediterranean. - Wilson.
BEIT-JEBRIN, a village of Syria, 6 hours WNW from Hebron, which Dr. Robinson identifies with the ancient Betogabris and Eleutheropolis.
BEIT-EL-MELE'K-EIDE, a village of Darfur, 100 m. SSW of Cobbe, on the road to Darkulla, in N lat. 12° 59'.
BEIT IBN MERI, a town of Arabia, in Yemen, 38 m. N of Chamir.
BEIT IBN NASR, a town of Arabia, in Yemen,
BEIT IBN SHEMSHAR, a town of Arabia, in Yemen, 28 m. E of Abu Arisch.
BEIT IL TOBA, a town of Arabia, in Yemen, 28 m. S of Saade.

BEITH, a parish and town in Ayrshire, in the district of Cunningham. Area 11,000 Scotch acres. Pop. 5,795, of whom 45 were in a small portion of the p. lying in Renfrewshire.—The town of B., 11 m. W of Paisley, 12 m. S of Largs, and 5 m. E of Dalry, has a pop. of above 3,000. Thread-making, cottonweaving, flax-spinning, and tanning, are the principal manufactures of the place. The Glasgow and Ayr railway passes within a mile of the town.

BEITIN, or BEITIL, a ruined village of Syria, 21 m. NE of Bireh, at which are extensive ruins supposed to be the remains of the ancient Bethel. The ruins principally lie at the extremity of a low ridge, with a slight shelving on each side, and surrounded by higher ground. Dr. Wilson remarked among them, a tank, constructed in regular Indian fashion, and measuring 100 by 75 paces.
BEITJALA, a village of Syria, 10 m. SW of Jerusalem, which Dr. Wilson conceives may have been the Zelah of Joshua.
BEITSTAD, a town of Norway, on the Beitstadfiorden, a branch of the Drontheimerfiorden. Pop. 2,700.
BEITUL, a town of India, in Gundwana, in N lat. 21° 55'. The ruins of Karim are in the vicinity.
BEIYA, a district of Persia N of Shiraz, and W of the Merdasht plain, watered by the Tezeng or Alai. It is highly fertile in wheat and barley.
BEJA. See BEDJA.

BE'JA, or Bexa, a district of Portugal, in the prov. of Alentejo, watered by the Odiarca and the Freijo. It is about 60 m. in length from E to W; and 30 m. in breadth; and comprehends 1 city, 3 towns, and 21 parishes.—The city of B., which was raised to the rank of duchy by John II., has a pop. of 6,000. It is a town of some antiquity, and is the see of a bishop, suffragan of Compostella. It lies on the side of a hill, in a delightful tract of country, 72 m. SSE of Lisbon. The Pax Julia of the Romans stood a little to the E of the present city.
BEJAGUR, a district of Hindostan, in the N part of Candeish, between the rivers Nerbuddah and Taptee, about 150 m. in length, and from 20 m. to 36 m. in breadth. Its cap. is Kurgun.

BEJAPORAM, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Orissa, 90 m. NW from Vizagapatam. BEJAPORE, BIJAPUR, or VISIAPUR, a province of the Deccan, in Hindostan, lying between the parallels of 15° and 18° N., and bounded on the N and E by the prov. of Aurungabad and Beder; on the S by the Balaghat, Canara, and the river Tumbudra; and on the W by the Indian ocean. It is about 330 m. in length, and about 200 m. in breadth, and thus may be compared to England in size. It is watered by many fine rivers, the chief of which are the Tumbudra, the Crishna, the Beemah, and the Gutputba. A great chain of mountains, a continuation of the Western Ghauts, traverses the country at a distance of 40 or 60 m. from the sea, and produces a sensible effect on the climate. The passes leading through this chain to the low land of the coast are very difficult, and most are defended by fortresses of great strength. In general, the soil is fertile, and provisions are plentiful and cheap; horses, forming the best cavalry equipment of the Mahrattas, are reared on the banks of the Beemah. The prov. is divided into 16 districts: viz., the Concan, Colapur, Mortizabad, Assodnagur, B., Sockur, Raichur, Mudgal, Anagundi, Gujundegur, Bancapur, Gunduck, Nurgul, Azimnagur, Raibagh, and Darwar.—The principal towns are B., Bijanagur, Colapur, Darwar, Goa, Hubli, Marichi, BEJ Satara, Shahnur, and Wari, Hattani, Copal, and Bancapur.—Bejapur was formerly a great and independent kingdom, under a dynasty founded by Adil Shah after the dissolution of the Bhamanee empire. Fourfifths of it were claimed by the Mahrattas until 1818; the rest is subject to the Nizam. Its pop. is calculated at 7,000,000, of whom the Mahommedans constitute a twentieth part; the remainder are Hindus.

BEJAPUR, or VISIAPUR, the cap. of the above prov., is situated in a fertile plain, in N lat. 16° 46', E long. 75° 47', near the r. bank of a tributary of the Crishna. It is a place of great extent, consisting of three towns within each other,-the innermost of which, the citadel, is a mile in circuit; the next is said to be not less than 8 m. in compass. The exterior town is environed by a wall many miles in circumf.; but a great proportion of the enclosed space is covered with jungle and ruins, and nothing to be depended on is known with respect to the number of the population. Yet, at a little distance, B. does not reveal the ruin and desolation which reign within: an immense assemblage of towers, domes, pinnacles, and spires, shoot up into the sky, partially intermingled with tamarind and other trees. The inhabitants affirm, that during the period of its greatest prosperity, B. contained 984,456 houses, and 1,600 mosques; and it is said that when invested by Aurungzebe, in 1689, 15,000 cavalry could encamp between the fort and the city wall. The fort is protected by high walls, strengthened by massy towers, and is surrounded by a ditch. It has seven gates, five of which are in use; and it contains several cannon of enormous dimensions. One, in particular, called the Mulk-e-Meidan, or 'Sovereign of the Plains,' weighs 40 tons. The citadel is a place of very great strength; and is encompassed by a ditch in most places 100 yards wide. Its works are constructed of the most massy materials; and it contains the palaces of the kings, one of which is built of fine black stone. Several mosques and mausoleums, adorned with all the embellishments of Eastern architecture, are to be seen in Bejapur, particularly one commenced by Adil Shah. Another in a different part of the city, built by Ibrahim, about 1620, is said to have cost £700,000, and to have occupied 6,533 workmen, during 36 years, 11 months, and 11 days. The great dome of Mahommed Shah's tomb is visible 14 m. distant, at the village of Kunnu. The fretwork on the ceilings and verandas, the panels covered with passages of the Koran in bas-relief, and stone trellises pierced with a meshwork of Arabic characters, which occur in the buildings at B., are all in the highest style of Oriental sculpture. The rich overlapping cornices and small minarets, terminating in a globe or pinnacle, instead of square, open turrets, are peculiar and remarkable features of these ruins. In the fort several buildings still remain in a good state of preservation. There is one entire and very regular street nearly 3 m. in length and 50 ft. wide, paved throughout, and presenting many stone buildings both public and private. The military khajus, or treasury, is a great curiosity, on account of the massy stone-chains that hang from its angles, in the links of which no joinings are perceptible.—Bejapur was besieged by Aurungzebe, and surrendered by capitulation in 1689. The city and the surrounding district are inhabited chiefly by Canarese, who assisted the British, under Sir Thomas Munro, in expelling the Mahrattas. Religious mendicants, chiefly Mahommedans, are numerous in this city.—The district of Bijapur is bounded on the N by the Beemah or Bheema river; on the E by the district of Sackur or Sacur; on the S by the Crishna; and on the W by the Man and the Angari.

BEJAR, or BEXAR, a partido and town in Spain, アクタンノフンンメイトメントトルストストストストストストストストストストストストストストストストスト in the division of Salamanca, in Leon, in a valley formed by the high mountains of the Sierra de Bejar, near the Cuerpo del Hombre river. Pop. 4,700. Cloth is manufactured here; and the hams of B. are renowned.

BEJETSK, a town of Russia, in the gov. and 60 m. NNE of Twer, near the Mologa. Pop. 4,000. An annual fair is held here, at which considerable quantities of grain, iron, silk and cotton stuffs, are disposed of.—The pop. of the district of B. in 1836 was 139,439.
BEJO, a village of Spain, in Galicia, 12 m. S of Santiago. There is a mineral spring in the neighbourhood.
BEJUCAL, a town of Cuba, in the jurisdiction and 25 m. S of Havannah.
BEK, a river of Servia, rising in Mount Haiduk, to the E of Zagulietza, and flowing from S to N to the Danube, which it enters near Gradistie, after a course of 60 m.

BEKAA (EL), a valley of Syria, in the pash. of Damascus, extending in a NE direction between the Antilibanus and Libanus, from the sea-coast to Baalbec. It is several miles broad, well cultivated, and covered with villages and mulberry plantations. That portion which lies to the NE of the Nahr Berdun, a small stream near Zahle, is distinguished as the Belled Baalbec. The Bekaa is principally inhabited by Turks; about one-fifth of the pop. are Greek Catholics. The usual produce of the harvest on this plain is ten-fold, but in fruitful years, twenty-fold. About five-sixths of the soil are left in pasture for the Arabs.—Burckhardt.—Lindsay.—Pococke.
BEKENRIED, or BECKENRIED, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Unterwalden, 5 m. E of Stanz, on the lake of the Four cantons. Pop. 1,100.

BE'KE'S, or Bekesch, a county in E Hungary, bounded on the NE and E by the co. of Bihar; on the SE and S by Arad and Tsanad; on the W by Tsongrad and Heves; and on the NW by Great Cumani. It is about 45 m. in its greatest length and greatest breadth. Its superficies is 65.4 German, or 1,388 English sq. m. Its soil is fertile; but the prevailing level of the surface renders it marshy and the climate unhealthy. Its principal stream is the Körös or Körösch. The principal productions are cattle, sheep, cheese, wool, wheat, and honey. The pop. in 1837 was 149,792, of whom a great majority were Magyars or Hungarians; and the remaining portion chiefly slaves. According to religions this pop. was divided into 40,000 of the Reformed Evangelical church, 35,000 Lutherans, and 14,000 Catholics; the remainder were chiefly members of the Greek church.—The district comprises 5 towns and 15 villages; and is divided into the two circles of Gyula on the E, and Csaba on the W.—The town of B., the cap. of the comitat, is situated at the confluence of the White and the Black Körös, 10 m. NW of Gyula, in N lat. 46° 46′ 16″, E long. 21° 7′. Pop. 14,800. It has a considerable cattle-market. In its environs are the remains of the ancient castle which originally gave name to the district.
BEKHOF, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Courland, W of Mitau.

BEKIA, or BECQUIA, one of the Granadines, between the islands of Granada and St. Vincent. On its W side is a good sandy bay, called Admiralty bay, where ships may ride, and wood and water can be obtained and fish. The position of the small v. on this bay is N lat. 13° 1', W long. 61° 15'. B. was called Little Martinico by the French. It is princicipally frequented for the purpose of catching turtle. It produces wild cotton and water-melons, but is infested with vipers, snakes, and venomous insects. It is 60 m. NE of Granada, and 99 WSW of Barbados. | the Crimea, rising in the lofty table-land of the Yaila BEL

BELA, a town of Beluchistan, in the prov. of Lus, in N lat. 26° 9'. Pop. 5,000. It is near the Puralli river.—Also a town of Hungary, in the circle of Zips, 12 m. NNW of Leutschau, on the l. bank of the Poprad. Pop. 2,782 Lutherans. It has a trade in wine, tobacco, and iron.-Also a town of Bulgaria, on the r. bank of an affluent of the Jantra, half way between Ternova and Rustchuk.
BELAIA. See BIELAYA.

BELABRE, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Indre, arrond. of Le Blanc.—The cant., comprising 7 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 8,681.— The com. 8 m. SE of Le Blanc, on the Anglain, has a pop. of 2,000. There are iron works here.

BELAIA-TSERKOV, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Kiev, and the district of Vasilkov, on the l. bank of the Ros, 16 m. NW of Taratsha. It is remarkable for a victory gained here by the Poles over the Prekop Tartars in 1626.

BELAIR, a village in the state of Maryland, U.S., the cap. of Harford co., 53 m. N by E of Annapolis. Pop. 150.—Also a v. in Lancaster district, South Carolina, 90 m. N by E of Columbia.—Also a v. in Richmond co., Georgia.
BELAKAN, or BELAKANI, a district of Georgia, on the NE frontier of Kakheti, 80 m. SW of Derbend.
BELAN, a parish in co. Kildare, 3 m. SSW of Ballytore. Area 1,198 acres. Pop. 286.
BELANAGRE, a village in the p. of Kilcorkey, in co. Roscommon, 2 m. SE by S of Frenchpark. Pop. 209.
BELAN-SUR-OURCE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cote-d'Or, arrond. and 9 m. NNE of Chatillon-sur-Seine. Pop. 806. There are iron-works here.
